channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Power
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] FortiBleed: 430,000 Fortinet firewalls swept for 110M+ credentials, now tied to active ransomware — the remote-access doorway into utility OT
item.description: Researchers now tie the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign directly to active ransomware. On 2 July, SOCRadar reported the operation — which planted a custom sniffer on roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls across 150+ countries and harvested more than 110 million credentials since mid-June — is feeding INC Ransom and Lynx intrusions, with at least a dozen ransomware deployments traced to the stolen access. For electric utilities the exposure is direct: these firewalls front remote access into EMS/SCADA, substations and the NERC CIP electronic security perimeter. Because valid credentials leaked rather than a single bug being exploited, no patch closes the gap, and an attacker signing in looks like a legitimate engineer. Treat any exposed FortiGate guarding OT as already compromised. - The Hacker News / SOCRadar - Ransomware link reported 2 Jul 2026 (campaign uncovered mid-Jun)

item.title: [High] China-linked CL-STA-1062 deploys TinyRCT backdoor in electricity and water utilities across Southeast Asia
item.description: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 reported in late June that a Chinese-speaking group it tracks as CL-STA-1062 has expanded from Taiwanese web-hosting targets into Southeast Asian critical infrastructure, compromising electricity and water utilities alongside government and military organizations. The group plants a lightweight C# backdoor named TinyRCT for persistence, command execution, file exfiltration and screenshotting, with a self-destruct feature to erase forensic evidence. For grid operators the takeaway is strategic pre-positioning: state actors are quietly establishing footholds in utility IT that can pivot toward OT, so hunt for stealthy persistence rather than waiting for disruption. - The Hacker News / Unit 42 - Reported late Jun 2026

item.title: [High] U.S. agencies keep warning Iran-affiliated actors are logging into internet-exposed PLCs across energy and water
item.description: The April joint advisory AA26-097A from CISA, FBI, NSA, EPA, DOE and Cyber Command — that Iranian-affiliated actors are reaching internet-facing programmable logic controllers across energy, water and government facilities — remains an active campaign, re-emphasized amid Middle East tensions. For power operators the lesson maps onto distributed generation and auxiliary systems, where solar sites, storage and small substations increasingly expose controllers reachable from the public internet, often shielded only by default or shared credentials. The activity is agency-confirmed rather than a hacktivist boast and has produced real operational disruption. The remedy is hygiene: get controllers off the public internet, replace default credentials, and hunt for unexpected logic changes. - CISA / FBI / NSA / EPA / DOE (AA26-097A) - Joint advisory Apr 2026 — campaign ongoing

item.title: [High] Dragos: KAMACITE mapped U.S. grid control loops for ELECTRUM — and VOLTZITE has operated inside a U.S. electric utility
item.description: Dragos' 2026 electric-grid threat reporting describes KAMACITE, the access team enabling the grid-focused group ELECTRUM, systematically scanning internet-exposed HMIs, variable-frequency drives, meters and cellular gateways across the United States to map entire control loops. Separately, Dragos says VOLTZITE — which receives footholds from the initial-access group SYLVANITE — has been observed operating inside a U.S. electric utility. The through-line is that adversaries are investing in patient reconnaissance of the grid edge rather than burning novel exploits, so reducing internet exposure of field devices and watching for reconnaissance is where detection pays off. - Dragos - 2026 electric-grid threat reporting

item.title: [High] ELECTRUM's attack on Poland's distributed energy resources is the template for grid-edge risk
item.description: Dragos attributes a December 2025 attack on operational-technology systems across multiple Polish grid sites to ELECTRUM, calling it the first major cyberattack focused on distributed energy resources — wind, solar and combined-heat-and-power. The intrusion targeted the RTUs and communications managing dozens of smaller generation sites, precisely the edge that expands as grids add renewables. For North American operators building out DER it is a preview: the distributed edge multiplies remotely reachable controllers faster than most utilities can monitor them, and destructive intent is now demonstrated rather than theoretical. - Dragos / Industrial Cyber - Attack Dec 2025 — foundational context

item.title: [High] CISA flags ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals for the energy sector
item.description: Among six ICS advisories CISA released on 2 July (ICSA-26-183-01) was one for ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals, called out for energy-sector use. Two API flaws let an unauthenticated attacker on the network read sensitive device data (CVE-2026-38059) and force a reboot via cross-site request (CVE-2026-38057), which repeated can sustain a link outage. Satellite backhaul is how many remote substations and DER sites phone home, so a vulnerable terminal is a foothold — and a denial-of-service risk — at the very edge of the grid. iDirect fixed it in software 4.5.2.2. - CISA ICS Advisories - Advisory 2 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] CISA batch flags ICS vulnerabilities across GE Vernova, Hitachi Energy, Siemens, Schneider and Mitsubishi
item.description: A June CISA advisory cycle covered vulnerabilities spanning grid-relevant vendors including GE Vernova and Hitachi Energy alongside Schneider Electric, Mitsubishi Electric and Delta. For power operators the point is less any single CVE than the cadence: the gear running substations, protection and energy management keeps generating advisories, and the vast majority ship with vendor fixes or mitigations. Triage by exposure and impact, patch the internet-reachable and safety-relevant systems first, and keep management interfaces off routable networks. - CISA / Industrial Cyber - CISA ICS advisories, Jun 2026

item.title: [Standard] NERC CIP-015-1 internal network security monitoring moves toward enforcement — blind spots inside the ESP close
item.description: With FERC's Order 907 approving NERC CIP-015-1, registered entities are building toward Internal Network Security Monitoring for high- and medium-impact BES Cyber Systems inside their Electronic Security Perimeters — a shift from perimeter defense to watching east-west traffic where intrusions like the grid-edge reconnaissance Dragos describes would show up. FERC has also directed NERC to extend the requirement to access-control and monitoring systems outside the ESP. Use the runway to deploy OT-aware monitoring, baseline normal control traffic, and close the visibility gap that leaves most incidents detected only by a hunch. - Dragos / NERC - FERC-approved; implementation ramping 2026
